text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Multidisciplinary Pancreatic Cancer Program — Treatment Planning Visit

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Gastrointestinal Oncology Attending]
SERVICE: Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Follow-up / Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Discussion / Treatment Planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African-descent female with borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma presenting today for post-treatment visit following completion of 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX). She is here for results review, toxicity assessment, tumor board discussion summary, and transition to the next phase of her neoadjuvant treatment plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old retired high school science teacher with a well-characterized diagnosis of moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head/uncinate process, confirmed by EUS-guided fine needle biopsy approximately 4 months ago. At the time of diagnosis, cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass with SMA abutment exceeding 180 degrees of circumferential contact and SMV lumen narrowing of approximately 40%, classifying the tumor as borderline resectable by NCCN criteria. PET-CT confirmed locoregional disease without distant metastasis. Molecular profiling (Tempus xT) revealed KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and SMAD4 wild-type (retained on IHC). TMB was low (3 mutations/Mb) and the tumor was microsatellite stable (MSS). No germline actionable variants were identified.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board consensus, the patient was initiated on neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX: oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced from standard 180 mg/m2 at initiation given patient age and comorbid diabetes), and 5-FU 2,400 mg/m2 as a 46-hour continuous infusion, administered on a biweekly schedule with G-CSF prophylaxis. The irinotecan dose was further reduced to 120 mg/m2 after cycle 3 due to grade 2 diarrhea. Over the course of 8 cycles, the patient experienced grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 2 fatigue, grade 2 nausea, grade 2 diarrhea, and grade 1 alopecia. She required no hospitalizations and her ECOG performance status remained 1 throughout.

At midpoint restaging after cycle 4, CA 19-9 had fallen from 1,200 U/mL to 380 U/mL and imaging demonstrated minor size reduction of the primary mass (3.4 to 3.0 cm) with some improvement in SMA abutment from >180 to approximately 160 degrees, prompting the decision to complete the full planned 8 cycles.

Today, she has completed all 8 cycles of mFOLFIRINOX. She reports her back pain has significantly improved and is now only intermittently present. Energy levels are slowly returning, though she notes ongoing fatigue that she rates as 4/10 in severity. She continues to experience bilateral fingertip and toe paresthesias consistent with cumulative oxaliplatin-associated neuropathy — she describes these as a persistent "buzzing" sensation, worsened by cold. She has been managing with warm gloves and avoiding cold beverages. Nausea has largely subsided since completing her last cycle approximately 3.5 weeks ago. Appetite has improved modestly; she has regained approximately 2 lbs since the cycle 4 visit. Bowel function is regular (1-2 formed stools daily, no diarrhea). She denies new-onset jaundice, fever, chills, or abdominal distension. She has not noted worsening of her epigastric discomfort. Her blood sugar management remains challenging — she continues on metformin and has required dose adjustments, with fasting glucoses now running in the 160-190 mg/dL range (improvement from 220-280 mg/dL at diagnosis, likely reflecting improved glycemic control with tumor response). HbA1c measured 3 weeks ago was 8.1%.

Restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol and MRI liver were performed after completion of cycle 8 and reviewed at today's visit. The pancreatic head/uncinate mass has decreased to 2.6 cm (from 3.4 cm at baseline, representing approximately a 24% reduction in longest diameter). The SMA abutment has decreased to approximately 120 degrees of circumferential contact (from >180 degrees at diagnosis). The SMV relationship has improved markedly, with only mild abutment and no significant luminal compromise (previously 40% narrowing). MRI liver showed no hepatic metastases. No peritoneal implants or pathologic lymphadenopathy were identified.

CA 19-9 has declined to 85 U/mL from a baseline of 1,200 U/mL — a 93% reduction. CEA was not repeated at this visit but was previously 5.8 ng/mL at diagnosis.

This case was formally re-presented at the multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board this week, attended by HPB surgical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional radiology, pathology, and medical oncology. The surgical oncologist reviewed the restaging imaging in detail. While the tumor has shown a meaningful response to mFOLFIRINOX — both radiographically and by tumor marker — the SMA abutment at 120 degrees remains above the threshold of <90 degrees that would generally be considered technically resectable with low risk of margin-positive resection. The consensus recommendation of the tumor board is to proceed with concurrent chemoradiation therapy for local consolidation, with the primary goal of further reducing SMA abutment and maximizing the probability of achieving a clear surgical margin (R0 resection) at the time of subsequent Whipple procedure. Long-course conventionally fractionated chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine was selected over SBRT-based approaches given the degree of vascular involvement and the desire for prolonged radiation exposure to address the tumor-vessel interface. The patient will be seen in radiation oncology to initiate treatment planning simulation.

The patient has been fully counseled on this plan. She understands the rationale and is agreeable. She has expressed a strong desire to pursue curative-intent treatment and is accepting of additional time on neoadjuvant therapy before surgery. She reports a robust support system at home including her daughter, who accompanies her to most visits.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Peripheral neuropathy (bilateral distal extremities, grade 2, persistent), fatigue (grade 2, improving), mild intermittent epigastric discomfort/back pain (improving significantly from baseline), decreased appetite (improving), mild hair thinning.

Negative: No jaundice, no pruritus, no dark urine or acholic stools, no fever, no chills, no dyspnea, no chest pain, no hematemesis, no melena or hematochezia, no new abdominal swelling, no lower extremity edema, no confusion or cognitive changes. No symptoms of bowel obstruction.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Pleasant, well-appearing, appropriately dressed African-descent female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversant and engaged.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 61.8 kg (up 2 lbs from nadir during treatment; down approximately 10 lbs from pre-diagnosis weight of ~68 kg). BMI approximately 23.2 kg/m2.

HEENT: Sclerae anicteric. No icterus. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis.

Skin: Mild diffuse alopecia (grade 1). No palmar-plantar erythema or desquamation. No jaundice or rash.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender in the epigastric region without guarding or rigidity. No palpable mass appreciated today (previously a vague epigastric firmness had been noted at diagnosis). No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly by palpation. No ascites. No visible distension. Bowel sounds active.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Bilateral distal extremity paresthesias noted on sensory testing: diminished light touch sensation at fingertips and toes bilaterally; proprioception and vibration sense mildly reduced at great toes bilaterally. No motor deficits. Gait steady.

Neurological: CN II-XII grossly intact. Deep tendon reflexes mildly diminished at Achilles bilaterally. No ataxia. Romberg negative.

Lymph Nodes: No axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CA 19-9: 85 U/mL (ULN 37 U/mL) — significant decline from baseline 1,200 U/mL; 93% reduction
CEA: Not repeated at this visit (previously 5.8 ng/mL at diagnosis, 3.9 ng/mL at cycle 4 midpoint restaging)
CBC:
  - WBC 4.8 x10^9/L (within normal limits)
  - Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (mild anemia, improved from nadir)
  - Platelets 224 x10^9/L
  - ANC 2.9 x10^9/L
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL (normalized from 1.1 mg/dL at diagnosis)
  - Alkaline phosphatase 88 U/mL (normalized from 142 U/mL at diagnosis)
  - ALT 32 U/mL (normalized)
  - AST 28 U/mL (normalized)
  - Albumin 3.7 g/dL
  - Creatinine 0.84 mg/dL; eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2
  - Glucose (fasting) 174 mg/dL
HbA1c (3 weeks prior): 8.1% (improved from 9.2% at diagnosis)
INR: 1.0
Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low-normal; patient instructed to supplement given prior oxaliplatin therapy)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (obtained after cycle 8 completion, reviewed today):
- Primary tumor: Pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measures 2.6 cm (was 3.0 cm at midpoint restaging after cycle 4, was 3.4 cm at diagnosis). The mass remains hypoattenuating with poorly defined margins, consistent with the known primary.
- SMA relationship: The tumor's contact with the superior mesenteric artery has decreased to approximately 120 degrees of circumferential abutment, compared to greater than 180 degrees at diagnosis and approximately 160 degrees after cycle 4. This is a meaningful and incremental improvement, though the tumor still contacts the SMA at greater than 90 degrees, which remains technically borderline for margin-negative resection.
- SMV relationship: The superior mesenteric vein now shows only mild abutment without significant luminal compromise, a substantial improvement from the 40% luminal narrowing seen at diagnosis. No venous occlusion.
- Portal vein confluence: No involvement.
- Celiac axis and hepatic artery: No involvement.
- Lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged peripancreatic or regional lymph nodes (largest node 7 mm, stable).
- Liver: No hepatic lesions.
- Peritoneum: No ascites or peritoneal deposits.

MRI liver with gadolinium (obtained concurrently for hepatic characterization, reviewed today):
- No hepatic lesions suspicious for metastasis.
- No biliary dilation.
- Post-procedure changes from prior EUS-FNB: none identified.

Overall imaging impression: Meaningful partial response to mFOLFIRINOX with approximately 24% reduction in tumor size and significant improvement in vascular relationships, particularly at the SMV. SMA abutment improved but persistent at approximately 120 degrees. No distant metastatic disease.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head/uncinate process, moderately differentiated (KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A deleted, SMAD4 wild-type, MSS, TMB-low), AJCC clinical stage T3 N0 M0 — now with meaningful partial response following 8 cycles neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX:
   - CA 19-9 down 93% from baseline (1,200 → 85 U/mL)
   - Primary tumor size reduced from 3.4 cm to 2.6 cm
   - SMA abutment improved from >180 degrees to approximately 120 degrees
   - SMV relationship markedly improved, no longer hemodynamically significant
   - No distant metastatic disease identified
   - Tumor remains borderline technically due to persistent SMA abutment at 120 degrees (goal <90 degrees for comfortable resection)

2. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 2, cumulative — ongoing bilateral distal paresthesias, worse with cold exposure. Given 8 cycles of oxaliplatin completed, no further oxaliplatin will be administered. Neuropathy expected to plateau and gradually improve over months following cessation.

3. Post-chemotherapy fatigue, grade 2, improving.

4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus with suboptimal glycemic control (HbA1c 8.1%), influenced by systemic inflammation from underlying PDAC, prior chemotherapy, and glucocorticoid antiemetic use. Improvement noted from diagnosis (HbA1c 9.2%). Continued metformin management; endocrinology co-management recommended prior to surgery.

5. Hypertension and hyperlipidemia — medications continued, clinically stable.

6. Mild normocytic anemia — likely multifactorial (chemotherapy-associated + chronic disease). No transfusion required. Iron studies to be rechecked.

---

PLAN

1. TRANSITION TO CHEMORADIATION: Per multidisciplinary tumor board consensus (HPB surgery, radiation oncology, and medical oncology in agreement), patient will proceed with long-course concurrent chemoradiotherapy for local consolidation of the primary tumor, with the specific aim of reducing SMA abutment below 90 degrees and maximizing the probability of an R0 resection at eventual surgery. Planned approach:
   - Radiation: Conventionally fractionated IMRT to the pancreatic head tumor, targeting 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction). Clinical target volume will include the primary tumor with a 5 mm margin; prophylactic nodal irradiation will not be performed given absence of nodal disease on imaging.
   - Concurrent chemotherapy: Capecitabine (Xeloda) 825 mg/m2 orally twice daily on radiation days as a radiosensitizer, consistent with standard practice. The dose was chosen to balance radiosensitization with tolerability given prior chemotherapy exposure.
   - Referral placed today to radiation oncology for CT simulation and treatment planning.
   - Anticipated treatment duration: approximately 5.5 weeks (28 fractions).

2. RESTAGING AFTER CHEMORADIATION: Approximately 4-6 weeks following completion of chemoradiation, restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol will be obtained to reassess the primary tumor and vascular relationships prior to surgical planning. CA 19-9 will be rechecked at that time.

3. SURGICAL PLANNING: HPB surgical oncology will remain actively engaged. If imaging after chemoradiation demonstrates adequate reduction in SMA abutment (ideally <90 degrees) and absence of metastatic disease, patient will be referred for pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) at a high-volume center. Surgical resection remains the primary curative goal.

4. NEUROPATHY MANAGEMENT: No additional oxaliplatin exposure. Patient counseled on cold avoidance, use of warm gloves, and physical therapy referral for neuropathy-focused exercises. Will reassess grade at each visit. Gabapentin discussed but patient prefers to defer unless symptoms worsen functionally.

5. GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT: Endocrinology co-management strongly recommended given anticipated worsening of diabetes during radiation therapy and before and after potential Whipple procedure (which may require further insulin adjustment due to loss of pancreatic tissue). Referral placed today. Continue metformin; glucose monitoring logs reviewed at next visit.

6. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT: Referral to oncology dietitian to support weight recovery and pre-surgical optimization of nutritional status. Patient counseled on high-protein, high-calorie dietary strategies. Pancreatic enzyme supplementation is not yet indicated (exocrine function intact at this time), but will be reassessed post-surgically.

7. LABS: CBC, CMP, CA 19-9, CEA to be rechecked at the start of chemoradiation and mid-treatment. Magnesium supplementation recommended given borderline-low level.

8. PERFORMANCE STATUS: ECOG 1. Patient is medically fit to proceed with chemoradiation and ultimately surgery.

9. GOALS OF CARE / PSYCHOSOCIAL: Patient and family remain well-informed and fully engaged in the treatment plan. She has clearly articulated her desire for curative-intent therapy and understands the stepwise nature of the neoadjuvant approach. Social work support has been offered; patient has declined at this time but may revisit. She has strong family support at home. Advance care planning documents are on file.

10. RETURN TO CLINIC: Patient will be seen in radiation oncology within the next 1-2 weeks for simulation and treatment planning. Follow-up in medical oncology is planned approximately 3-4 weeks into chemoradiation to assess tolerance and manage any treatment-related toxicities. She is instructed to call the oncology triage line for any concerning symptoms including fever (>38°C), severe nausea or vomiting, inability to eat or drink, or new abdominal pain.

---

Time spent with patient: Approximately 55 minutes, including reviewing imaging, discussing tumor board recommendations, counseling, and treatment planning.

Documented by: [Gastrointestinal Oncology Attending]
Co-signed: [GI Oncology Fellow]
